shell.title: Compare-first price intelligence for the real buying decision
shell.summary: Start with Compare Preview, keep the right candidate alive as a watch task or compare-aware watch group, then use runtime evidence, AI-assisted explanations, recovery guidance, and store/operator surfaces from one place.
shell.productShapeValue: URL -> Compare Preview -> WatchTask / WatchGroup -> Runs -> Recovery / Alerts / Store Ops
shell.secondaryHint: Detail views appear after you open or create a task or group.

compare.label: Current focus
compare.headline: Cleanly compare the basket before moving anything into watch state
compare.summary: Compare at least two product URLs first, let the decision board explain what is strong and what is still shaky, then decide between a watch group or evidence-only review.
compare.proofPath: Save a local evidence package first; only move into runtime packages or watch groups once the basket is honest enough.

watchNew.label: Current focus
watchNew.headline: Turn one confirmed row into executable watch state
watchNew.summary: This lane only matters after the compare result is honest enough. Confirm the title, candidate key, and recipient before you commit.
watchNew.proofPath: Keep the compare package first, then treat the one-row watch task as the final move.

watchList.label: Current focus
watchList.headline: Review which watches still need confirmation before adding more URLs
watchList.summary: If watches or groups are already queued, return to their evidence and cooldown state before starting another compare run.
watchList.proofPath: The watch list only consumes compare results that already stand on solid evidence; it does not replace compare itself.

settings.label: Current focus
settings.headline: Tune the reminder cadence into a trustworthy execution surface
settings.summary: This is not the primary lane, but it decides whether later alerts become noise. Confirm the recipient and cooldown before you go back to compare.
settings.proofPath: Settings enforce discipline; they do not create the decision itself.

form.title: Inspect cross-store URL candidates before you create a task or group
form.summary: Start small: paste the candidate URLs, let DealWatch normalize the basket, then use the decision board to decide whether this should stay in review, become one task, or become a compare-aware group.
form.productUrlsHelp: Use one URL per line. The compare preview accepts 2 to 10 non-empty URLs.

errors.invalidProductUrl: Each line must be a valid product URL.
errors.minUrls: Enter at least two product URLs.
errors.maxUrls: Enter no more than ten product URLs.

route.title: Keep the first screen about one decision, not six doors
route.summary: This page works best when you treat it like a guided desk: compare the rows first, read the decision board second, then commit only the watch state that the evidence actually supports.
route.stepCompareSummary: Paste the URLs and let the product tell you which rows really belong in the same candidate set.
route.stepDecisionSummary: Use the headline, risk summary, and strongest pair before you create anything durable.
route.stepCommitSummary: Create one watch task for a clear single row, or a watch group only when the basket truly holds together.

title.idle: Next move: generate a clean compare result
title.ready: Next move: follow the decision board

summary.idle: Submit at least two URLs first so the compare run can name the strongest row and the sharpest risk inside the basket.
summary.ready: Do not jump straight into watch creation. Use the decision board to decide whether this basket should stay in review, become a group, or stop at evidence.

title.local: Proof is saved locally and still reviewable
title.runtime: Proof already has a runtime-backed package

summary.empty: Save the evidence package before you commit anything. That way the proof survives even if the basket needs one more review pass.
summary.local: A local evidence package exists. Promote it only if the basket still stands up after review.
summary.runtime: This compare run already has a runtime-backed proof package, so the next move can stay honest without pretending the basket is final.

title.idle: This basket is not ready to commit into a group yet
title.ready: This basket can move toward a compare-aware watch group

summary.idle: This still behaves like a review lane, not a commit lane. Keep weaker rows in evidence instead of freezing them into long-lived watch state.
summary.ready: {{count}} rows are group-ready, so the group builder can become the final lane after the proof is preserved.

decision.summaryNote: The saved review package stays on this machine, and the runtime package stays local to this runtime. Neither action creates a hosted or public share link.
decision.savedTitle: Review saved compare packages without rerunning the compare
decision.savedSummary: Think of this as the evidence drawer. Save a compare snapshot once, then come back to the decision summary, risk note, and runtime review entry later.
decision.noSavedPackages: No saved compare evidence packages yet. Save one after a compare run if you want a local review surface.
decision.riskUnsupported: {{count}} submitted URL{{plural}} did not map to a supported store path.
decision.riskFetchFailed: {{count}} supported row{{plural}} still failed to fetch live offer evidence.
decision.riskStoreDisabled: {{count}} row{{plural}} belongs to a store that is currently disabled in runtime settings.
decision.riskNoPairScore: Multiple rows resolved, but none of them produced a pairwise compare score yet.
decision.riskWeakStrongest: The strongest pair is only {{score}}, so the rows may still describe different products.
decision.riskNeedTwoCandidates: A compare-aware watch group needs at least two successful candidates.
decision.riskLowerConfidence: {{count}} lower-confidence pair{{plural}} still deserve a quick human review before you trust the basket.
decision.riskNoBlocker: No obvious compare blocker surfaced in this preview, but the final decision still belongs to the operator.

state.noDecisionSummary: Nothing resolved into a reusable candidate, so this compare run should stay in review instead of creating watch state.
state.noDecisionAction: Fix the unsupported or fetch-failed rows before you create any watch task or watch group.
state.oneCandidateHeadline: One candidate is usable, but the compare basket is incomplete
state.oneCandidateSummary: {{bestCandidateLabel}} is the only row with durable evidence right now, so there is no honest cross-store winner yet.
state.oneCandidateAction: Create a single watch task only if this row already looks correct; otherwise gather more evidence first.
state.strongHeadline: This compare run is strong enough for a watch group
state.strongSummary: {{bestCandidateLabel}} currently looks like the clearest low-price row, and {{bestMatchLabel}} already forms a strong compare pair.
state.strongAction: Save the evidence package, then create a compare-aware watch group so the runtime can keep reranking instead of freezing one URL forever.
state.reviewHeadline: The basket is promising, but the decision still needs review
state.reviewSummary: {{bestCandidateLabel}} looks usable and {{bestMatchLabel}} is plausible, but the why-like / why-unlike notes still matter before you lock durable group state.
state.reviewAction: Review the evidence pair details, then decide whether this should become a watch group or stay as a single-task handoff.
state.mixedHeadline: The compare result is mixed and should stay in review
state.mixedSummary: {{bestCandidateLabel}} is still the clearest row, but the strongest pair is weak enough that a group could hide real product mismatch risk.
state.mixedAction: Keep this as evidence only, or create a single watch task from the clearest row after a manual check.

stats.aiNote: The deterministic decision board stays primary. This card is only the explain layer on top of that evidence.

summary.disabled: AI assistance is disabled for this workspace. The decision board, risk summary, and pair evidence remain the source of truth.
summary.error: AI-assisted explanation could not be generated for this compare run. Review the deterministic summary and pair evidence instead.
summary.skipped: AI-assisted explanation was skipped for this compare run. The deterministic summary and pair evidence still tell the full story.
summary.unavailable: AI-assisted explanation unavailable. Showing deterministic summary and evidence only.

notices.compareFailed: Failed to compare these product URLs.
notices.saveLocal: Saved this compare evidence package for local review on this machine.
notices.runtimeCreated: Runtime evidence package created. This stays local to the runtime and does not create a public link.
notices.runtimeFailed: Failed to create the runtime evidence package.
notices.copiedSummary: Copied the compare evidence summary. It describes the evidence package without pretending there is a public share link.
notices.clipboardFailed: Clipboard access failed. Save the package first and review it locally instead.
notices.needTwoCandidates: At least two successful compare candidates are required to create a watch group.
notices.createGroupFailed: Failed to create the watch group from compare candidates.

savedPanel.detailSummary: Use a saved package when you want the compare conclusion to stay reviewable on this machine before you turn it into longer-lived runtime evidence.

candidatePanel.intro: Once a row looks right, you can either carry one candidate into a single watch task, or keep several strong rows together in a compare-aware watch group.
candidatePanel.fetchedSummary: This row has enough live offer evidence to move into the watch flow.
candidatePanel.officialFullSummary: This row is on the full official product path. Compare intake, watch creation, compare-aware groups, recovery, and cashback-aware review are all available.
candidatePanel.officialPartialSummary: This row is on an official partial store path. Compare intake is real, but some broader store capabilities are still intentionally deferred.
candidatePanel.fetchFailedSummary: The URL is supported, but the fetch still failed, so this row should not drive a durable decision yet.
candidatePanel.unsupportedSummary: This URL did not resolve into a supported compare target.
candidatePanel.unsupportedHostSummary: This host is not in the official registry yet. DealWatch can keep it in compare review and repo-local evidence, but not as live watch state.
candidatePanel.unsupportedPathSummary: The store host is recognized, but this URL shape is not an officially supported product path yet.
candidatePanel.storeDisabledSummary: The store exists in the official registry, but its runtime binding is disabled, so this row stays evidence-only for now.
candidatePanel.recommendedCtaSummary: Use this when you want to turn one confirmed row into a watch task without carrying the whole basket forward.
candidatePanel.createWatchTaskFromRow: Create watch task from this row
candidatePanel.noDurableCta: No durable CTA yet. This row needs a successful fetch before it can become a watch task or count toward a group.

groupBuilder.title: Turn the strongest rows into one decision basket
groupBuilder.summary: A watch group is the product answer to "don't forget the alternatives." Instead of freezing one URL forever, the runtime can keep reevaluating the basket each time it runs.
groupBuilder.thresholdHelp: Suggested from the best currently fetched candidate so you start from real compare evidence.
groupBuilder.rowsEnteringBasket: Rows that will enter the basket
groupBuilder.noSuccessfulCandidates: No successful compare candidates are available yet, so group creation stays locked.
groupBuilder.lockedHint: A compare-aware group stays locked until at least two successful candidates survive the preview. That keeps a thin basket from pretending it is durable compare truth.
groupBuilder.creating: Creating watch group from compare candidates...
groupBuilder.createButton: Create watch group from successful candidates

errors.titleTooLong: Group title must stay within 120 characters.
errors.cadenceMin: Cadence must be at least 5 minutes.
errors.thresholdValueMin: Threshold value must be zero or above.
errors.cooldownMin: Cooldown must be zero or above.

pairEvidence.title: Pair evidence
pairEvidence.summary: These scores are the pair-by-pair proof. They explain why two rows look close, and why a row might still stay out of the basket.
pairEvidence.noPairScore: No successful pair produced a match score.

headline.error: AI recovery copilot hit an error

ai.panelNote: Use this as the readable recovery layer for the current group item. The deterministic reason and recommended action still stay primary.

summary.ok: This layer suggests a human-friendly recovery read of the current issue. The deterministic reason and recommended action stay primary.
summary.disabled: AI assistance is disabled for this workspace. The deterministic recovery reason and recommended action remain the evidence anchor.
summary.error: AI recovery copilot could not be generated for this group item. Use the deterministic reason and recommended action instead.
summary.skipped: AI recovery copilot was skipped for this group item. The deterministic reason and recommended action still describe what to do next.
summary.unavailable: AI recovery copilot unavailable. Keeping the deterministic recovery reason and recommended action as the evidence anchor.

recovery.title: Needs Attention / Recovery Inbox
recovery.summary: This is the operator triage lane. It separates single watch tasks from compare-aware groups so you can see what is blocked, why it slipped, and where to inspect next.
recovery.error: Failed to load the recovery inbox.
recovery.emptyTitle: No recovery work queued right now
recovery.emptySummary: Nothing is asking for manual intervention. That is good news, but it is not a fake green light for runtime readiness. Use Compare Preview when you want to create the next task or group.
recovery.noSingleTasks: No single-task recovery items right now.
recovery.noGroupItems: No compare-aware group recovery items right now.

taskList.error: Failed to load the control board.
taskList.emptySummary: The control board is still empty. Start with Compare Preview, then create either a single watch task or a compare-aware watch group.
taskList.newShapeBullet1: 1. A watch task tracks one resolved product target.
taskList.newShapeBullet2: 2. A watch group keeps multiple candidates alive and picks the best current option.
taskList.newShapeBullet3: 3. Health, backoff, and operator attention are now first-class runtime signals.
taskList.singleWatchSummary: These are the single-target monitoring contracts. They are still useful when one product URL is the right long-lived target.
taskList.groupsSummary: Think of a watch group as a candidate basket. Instead of tracking just one URL, it keeps multiple candidates alive and picks the best current option each run.
taskList.controlNotesBullet1: 1. `Healthy` means the monitor can keep running on schedule without operator help.
taskList.controlNotesBullet2: 2. `Degraded` means the monitor still runs, but recent failures or delivery issues lowered confidence.
taskList.controlNotesBullet3: 3. `Blocked` or `Needs Attention` means the system wants a human to intervene before trusting automation again.
taskList.controlNotesBullet4: 4. Watch groups are where compare-first becomes long-lived decision logic, not just a one-time intake step.

board.noGroups: No compare-aware groups yet. Build one from a successful Compare Preview result.

runtime.title: Runtime Readiness
runtime.summary: Think of this as the cockpit preflight card. It tells you what looks ready, what still needs operator work, and where there is simply no local evidence yet, so the UI does not pretend the runtime is green.
runtime.emptySummary: The backend did not return any runtime checks. That means there is no evidence to show here yet, not that the runtime is ready.

page.notificationsSummary: Set the default recipient lane here, then use the event feed below to confirm what actually left the runtime.
page.deliveryEventsSummary: This is the global notification operations view. It explains what happened to recent deliveries, not just how the rules are configured.
page.whyMcpBoundarySummary: This cockpit is where builder-safe read surfaces and maintainer reality meet. It explains what the runtime can expose safely today without pretending write-side automation is part of the public promise.
page.registryHealthySummary: Registry and cockpit signals line up right now, so the store matrix reads like one truthful control surface instead of two competing ledgers.
page.registryWarningSummary: Some store registry truth still needs review. Treat the cockpit as the lead view, then inspect the linked source refs before you widen store claims.
page.cockpitSummary: This is the store growth dashboard, not a decorative catalog. The table uses the live backend cockpit response, which already merges capability truth, runtime enablement, and onboarding contract evidence, so maintainers can see what is supported today and what a new store still owes before it becomes a real DealWatch product-path adapter.
page.cockpitBridgeSummary: The cockpit keeps capability truth, runtime enablement, and onboarding proof in one place so a store can earn product-path status instead of only looking supported on paper.
page.compareCapableCardSummary: These are the stores already shaped for compare-first intake instead of only single-link monitoring.
page.cashbackCardSummary: This count shows where effective-price context can join the same operator loop instead of living in a side spreadsheet.
page.disciplineCardSummary: The current rule is still prove the product path first, then widen store exposure. This keeps new store growth from outrunning evidence.
page.limitedSupportSummary: Unsupported hosts or unsupported product paths can still stay inside compare review and repo-local evidence, but they must not pretend to be live watch state.

notifications.recipientHelp: Use one operator inbox if you want one place to receive the default task and group alerts.
notifications.cooldownHelp: This is the quiet period after a send. It prevents one noisy task from spamming the recipient every few minutes.
notifications.enabledHelp: Turn this off when you want the runtime to keep collecting evidence without sending delivery events.

binding.fullLive: Live on the full official product path
binding.partialLive: Live on the current partial official path
binding.inProgressLive: Enabled for onboarding validation, not ready for broad store claims
binding.partialDisabled: Officially modeled, currently parked until more product-path proof lands
binding.liveCompareWatch: Live in compare and watch paths
binding.supportedDisabled: Supported by capability contract, currently disabled
binding.disabledUntilReady: Disabled until the product path is ready

cockpit.updateError: Failed to update store runtime switch.
cockpit.verificationCommandsDetail: These are the prove-it commands from the onboarding contract. In everyday terms: this is the exam sheet a new store has to pass before it earns shelf space.
cockpit.sourceRefsDetail: No second store truth is hidden in this page. These refs are the files and endpoint that define what the matrix means.

groupDetail.summaryTemplate: {{count}} candidate{{plural}} stay in this basket so the runtime can keep comparing instead of freezing the first decent row it sees.

hero.operationalSummary: This is the long-lived compare basket view: one place to judge the winner, the runner-up, and whether the basket still deserves operator trust.

groupDetail.winnerVsRunnerUpSummary: This is the head-to-head zone. It makes the current winner and the closest challenger visible in one place instead of burying the decision inside a long field list.
groupDetail.candidateBasketSummary: This is the long-lived compare context. The winner stays visible, but the alternatives never disappear from the decision surface.
groupDetail.riskSummary: This is the truth-telling column. It keeps operational risk visible so the winner card never makes the basket look safer than it really is.
groupDetail.runError: Group run failed. Review the latest group run for details.
groupDetail.error: Failed to load watch group detail.

headline.error: AI decision explanation hit an error

summary.ok: This layer explains the current winner in plain language. The deterministic decision, runner-up comparison, and risk notes still lead the page.
summary.disabled: AI assistance is disabled for this workspace. Current decision, winner vs runner-up, and deterministic risk notes remain the source of truth.
summary.error: AI decision explanation could not be generated for this group. Review the deterministic decision and risk notes instead.
summary.skipped: AI decision explanation was skipped for this group. The deterministic decision and risk notes still describe the basket state.
summary.unavailable: AI decision explanation unavailable. Showing deterministic winner, runner-up, and risk notes only.

aiPanel.title: AI decision read
aiPanel.note: Use this as the readable explanation layer for the current basket. Deterministic winner, runner-up, and risk notes still stay primary.

recentRuns.summary: This is the basket flight recorder: each run shows whether the current winner was earned cleanly or still needs another deliberate look.

comparison.noWinner: No current winner has been established yet.
comparison.noRunnerUp: No runner-up is available yet, so the basket still lacks a clear second-place comparison.

notes.operatorReviewRequiredDetail: Automation is paused until someone inspects the latest failure and decides whether to rerun.
notes.noRunDetail: The group exists, but there is no run record yet, so there is still no current winner or reliability baseline.
notes.latestRunDirtyTitle: The latest run did not finish cleanly
notes.latestRunDirtyDetail: Latest run status is {{status}}, so the current decision should be treated as incomplete evidence.
notes.backoffDetail: The runtime is waiting until {{value}} before it will retry automatically.
notes.failuresDetail: {{count}} consecutive failure{{plural}} mean the next rerun should happen with intent, not by habit.
notes.failedMembersTitle: Some candidate rows failed inside the latest basket run
notes.failedMembersDetail: {{count}} member{{plural}} did not return clean evidence, so the winner may be correct but not fully contested.
notes.thinLeadDetail: A spread of {{value}} means the winner could flip on the next run with only a small price change.
notes.noRunnerUpDetail: The current winner has evidence, but the basket still lacks a clean second-place row for direct comparison.
notes.stableTitle: The current basket looks operationally stable
notes.stableDetailWithTime: Latest success was captured at {{value}} and no obvious reliability blocker is active.
notes.stableDetailWithoutTime: No obvious blocker is active right now.
notes.decisionReasonFallback: No explicit decision reason was returned for this run, so keep the deterministic winner card and risk notes in view before trusting the basket.

artifact.summary: This block shows what the latest task run actually captured, so operators can judge whether the proof bundle is complete enough to trust the alert.

latestSignal.fallbackDecisionReason: No extra decision note was returned, so treat this as the latest visible signal and use the task health panel before rerunning.

taskHealth.summary: Treat this as the task's operating-room view: it tells you whether automation is healthy, drifting, or waiting for a deliberate retry.
taskHealth.operatorReviewDetail: Automation already decided this task should not keep self-retrying. Review the last failure before reopening the lane.
taskHealth.backoffDetail: The runtime will not retry again until {{value}}, which gives the last failure time to clear before another attempt.
taskHealth.failuresTitle: Failure streak changed the risk level
taskHealth.failuresDetail: {{count}} consecutive failure{{plural}} mean the next rerun should be intentional, not just habitual button-mashing.
taskHealth.healthyDetail: Recent task telemetry looks stable enough that the next alert can be interpreted as a fresh signal rather than leftover damage.

sections.latestSignalSummary: This is the latest price move that would actually matter to an operator: what changed, by how much, and whether the signal looks like a genuine new low or suspicious drift.
sections.compareHandoffSummary: This keeps the link back to the compare intake that created the task, so the operator can recover the original candidate evidence instead of treating the task as an orphan.
sections.priceTimelineSummary: The chart is the long-view evidence: use it to decide whether the latest alert is a real pattern change or just one noisy scrape.
sections.cashbackSummary: Cashback stays secondary to price, but this panel shows whether the current task is carrying an extra savings layer or still lacks that quote.
sections.recentRunsSummary: Read the latest attempts here before rerunning. In plain terms: this is the black box recorder for the task lane.

errors.run: Task run failed. Review the latest task run for details.

comparisonPage.brandTagline: DealWatch is not just another single-link tracker.

comparisonPage.statusCompareSummary: The first product move is validation, not blind task creation.
comparisonPage.statusProofSummary: Public claims sit behind explicit repository evidence, proof pages, and verification gates.

comparisonPage.dealwatchSummary: You compare multiple URLs first, inspect normalized candidates, score inputs, and why-like / why-unlike reasons, then either create a single watch task or keep the candidates alive inside a compare-aware watch group.

comparePreviewPage.title: DealWatch Compare Preview | Compare First, Then Track
comparePreviewPage.description: Open a read-only sample compare preview, then choose Quick Start, Proof, or Builders depending on whether you want the local runtime, the verifier trail, or read-only agent wiring.
comparePreviewPage.brandTagline: The safest first step in the AI-enhanced product flow.
comparePreviewPage.heroTitle: Check the product target before you create durable state.
comparePreviewPage.heroSummary: Compare Preview exists because ordinary price trackers assume you already know the right product URL. DealWatch starts one step earlier: validate the candidate, compare across stores, inspect deterministic evidence plus AI-assisted explanation, and only then decide whether the row deserves a watch task or a compare-aware watch group.

buildersPage.distributionNote: These are builder-pack, native-bundle, and listing-prep materials layered behind the honest read-only route. Published marketplace listings and hosted control planes still remain outside this first slice.
buildersPage.mirrorSummary: GitHub Pages now also serves static JSON mirrors for the client starters, builder starter pack, and the full all-clients config bundle, so a machine reader can study the repo-owned contract before starting the local runtime.
buildersPage.mirrorNote: These static mirrors are public proof assets for prompt starters, skill cards, starter maps, and all-clients payloads; they are not a hosted runtime or a write-capable control plane.
buildersPage.catalogSummary: The public builders surface now also ships a static JSON catalog that mirrors the repo-owned client map without pretending the runtime itself is hosted.
buildersPage.catalogNote: Use it when an agent needs prompt, skill, recipe, and example links in one read before starting the local runtime.
buildersPage.boundarySummary: A good builder frontdoor says both what to open next and what not to assume yet, so the page does not quietly turn into plugin theater.
buildersPage.boundaryPluginSummary: DealWatch now ships repo-owned builder-pack materials for every client, native bundle candidates for Claude Code and Codex, and listing-prep inputs for the broader ecosystem. That still does not mean any official marketplace or registry listing already exists.
buildersPage.boundaryHostedSummary: These builder paths are local-first. They do not prove a hosted remote control plane is live today.
buildersPage.boundaryWriteSummary: The current builder surface is read-only-first. Maintenance, cleanup, owner bootstrap, and maintainer-only browser debug commands stay outside the builder promise.
buildersPage.boundaryRecommendationSummary: DealWatch now ships a local Compare Preview recommendation slice, but builder surfaces still do not expose autonomous or builder-facing buy / wait recommendation support.

quickStartPage.title: DealWatch Quick Start | Local Runtime for Compare Preview, Watch Tasks, and Watch Groups
quickStartPage.description: Use the local runtime path after the static sample compare preview when you want to test your own grocery URLs.
quickStartPage.brandTagline: Move from Compare Preview into the local runtime.
quickStartPage.heroTitle: Run the local stack once you are ready to test your own URLs.
quickStartPage.heroSummary: Use Quick Start when you are ready to turn the sample product tour into a first real local win: your own URLs, real compare output, and the same evidence-first route. If you still need trust before setup, read Proof next. Builders stays the specialist route for coding-agent wiring.
quickStartPage.heroPrimaryCta: Jump to the local runtime commands
quickStartPage.heroVisualAlt: Compare Preview screen showing supported URLs, normalized results, and match scores
quickStartPage.layersTitle: Start with Compare Preview, then move into the local runtime
quickStartPage.layersSummary: Layer one is the sample compare preview. Layer two is the local stack for your own URLs. Builders is the separate route for coding-agent wiring. This keeps first contact lighter than a full manual setup wall.
quickStartPage.successTitle: What success looks like after the stack starts
quickStartPage.successSummary: The first local win is not "Docker started." It is "I can paste my own URLs, see normalized candidates and match reasons, and decide whether this should become durable watch state."
quickStartPage.successCardRouteTitle: You landed on the live compare route
quickStartPage.successCardRouteSummary: `http://localhost:5173/#compare` opens and the product still starts at Compare Preview instead of asking you to guess a durable task immediately.
quickStartPage.successCardEvidenceTitle: You can see real match reasons
quickStartPage.successCardEvidenceSummary: Your own URLs resolve into normalized candidates, scores, and why-like / why-unlike reasons instead of a blank setup page.
quickStartPage.successCardDecisionTitle: You know the next honest action
quickStartPage.successCardDecisionSummary: A good first run tells you whether to keep reviewing, create a watch task, or preserve compare context in a watch group. It does not ask you to trust the product blindly.
quickStartPage.step1Body:
Open Compare Preview
Click “Load sample compare”
Inspect match reasons
Remember: read-only, no data saved
quickStartPage.step3Title: 3. Open the live Compare Preview route after the local runtime is ready
quickStartPage.step4Body:
Use your own grocery product URLs,
or copy the public sample URLs from
the README quick-start section.
quickStartPage.manualSummary: Use this path if you want each process in its own terminal instead of the one-command Compose flow.
quickStartPage.pitfallsSummary: These are the three most common reasons a first local run feels harder than it should.
quickStartPage.pitfallSampleTitle: Treating the sample compare as live product data
quickStartPage.pitfallSampleSummary: The sample compare preview is static and read-only. It exists to show the first step quickly, not to verify your own URLs or create watch tasks or watch groups.
quickStartPage.pitfallDbSummary: Make sure your local environment points at a working database before you expect durable task state. The proof page and repo docs are the right places to confirm the current runtime contract.
quickStartPage.pitfallDockerTitle: Corrupted local Docker volume after an interrupted run
quickStartPage.pitfallDockerSummary: If the local database fails to start cleanly after an interrupted container run, reset the quickstart state with `docker compose down -v --remove-orphans` and then rerun `./scripts/quickstart_stack.sh -d --build`.
quickStartPage.pitfallWrongPageSummary: Do not start by manually creating a task unless you already know the right target URL. Start at Compare Preview first.
quickStartPage.pitfallReadonlySummary: Compare Preview is intentionally read-only. It validates candidate URLs before task creation, whether you are viewing the static sample or using the live local route.
quickStartPage.pitfallPartialTitle: Starting only part of the stack
quickStartPage.pitfallPartialSummary: The API alone is not the whole product loop. Run both runtime commands from the manual setup section if you want durable task state and recurring watch activity to behave normally.
quickStartPage.footerPrompt: Need the quickest no-install proof first?
quickStartPage.footerCompareCta: Go to the sample compare preview

schema.name: DealWatch Quick Start
schema.description: Use the local runtime path after the static sample compare preview when you want to compare your own grocery URLs.

step.name: Open the sample compare preview
step.text: Visit the Compare Preview page and load the static public sample. It is read-only and saves nothing.

step.name: Start the local runtime
step.text: Run ./scripts/quickstart_stack.sh when you are ready to compare your own URLs with the local API, worker, and WebUI.

step.name: Open the live Compare Preview route
step.text: After the local runtime is ready, open http://localhost:5173/#compare and paste your own URLs.

communityPage.title: DealWatch Community | Discussions, Roadmap, and Store Requests
communityPage.description: Find the right public entrypoint for DealWatch discussions, roadmap requests, store suggestions, support questions, and release updates.
communityPage.brandTagline: The public front desk for DealWatch.
communityPage.heroTitle: Where to go for questions, updates, and store requests
communityPage.heroSummary: The best community experience is not “open anything and hope.” It is knowing exactly where each kind of feedback belongs.
communityPage.cardStartSummary: The README start-here section is now the fastest way to understand where to begin as a new visitor without depending on an always-open issue.
communityPage.cardRoadmapSummary: The README roadmap section now holds the near-term direction: compare-aware watch groups, health/delivery closure, and next-store priorities, so open issues can stay reserved for real active backlog.
communityPage.cardUsageSummary: Use Discussions / Q&A when you need help understanding the product, setup, Compare Preview flow, watch groups, or notification behavior.
communityPage.cardBuildersSummary: Open the builder frontdoor when you want the shortest honest path for Codex, Claude Code, OpenClaw, or a similar coding-agent client.
communityPage.cardUpdatesSummary: Follow the release notes and product updates discussion if you want the public product surface to feel alive over time.
communityPage.cardStoresSummary: Use the dedicated Store request issue template when proposing a new merchant or store target. The remote checklist also expects the `store-request` label to stay present so the intake path does not drift from the public issue flow.
communityPage.cardStoriesSummary: Share how you use Compare Preview or compare-aware watch groups in the public discussion thread so the product story stays grounded in real workflows.
communityPage.settingsSummary: Some public trust signals still live in GitHub settings, not in tracked source files. Some can be verified with a token-aware script, while others still require a GitHub admin to confirm them in the UI.
communityPage.settingsAboutSummary: Keep the repository description, homepage, and topics aligned with the public product narrative. The remote checklist now prints current API-visible state instead of only repeating the intended contract.
communityPage.settingsSocialSummary: Upload `assets/social/social-preview-1280x640.png` in the GitHub repository settings UI. The repo can verify the asset file itself, but not whether GitHub is currently using that uploaded image.
communityPage.settingsCodeTitle: Code scanning findings (credentialed or manual check)
communityPage.settingsCodeSummary: With `GITHUB_TOKEN`, the remote verifier can confirm the current code-scanning alert count. Without credentials, this stays a blind spot and still needs GitHub UI review.
communityPage.settingsProtectedSummary: Keep `main` protected and retain the current required status checks that guard the public surface: `governance`, `test`, `frontend`, `product-smoke`, `CodeQL`, and `secret-hygiene`.
communityPage.settingsLabelsSummary: The remote verifier now checks that `store-request`, `compare-preview`, `public-surface`, and `release` exist instead of assuming they are already there.
communityPage.settingsTrustSummary: Discussions, GitHub Pages, and private vulnerability reporting should all stay enabled.
communityPage.settingsCommandSummary: Run `python3 scripts/print_remote_repo_settings_checklist.py` for the three-layer view: expected contract, current remote facts, and manual-only checks. Add `GITHUB_TOKEN=... python3 scripts/verify_remote_github_state.py` when you want credentialed remote verification instead of contract-only review.
communityPage.settingsReadmeSummary: Keep the README start-here and roadmap sections current so public onboarding does not rely on permanently open issues.
